[[JA:大井神社 (亀岡市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11432213}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Ooi Shrine | image = [[File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), entrance.jpg|260px]]
Torii | location = {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996}} Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|53.5|N|135|33|25.0|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ooi Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} | founded = (trad.) Wado 3 (710) | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963}} | rituals = Tachibana rite ({{ill|lt=August 19|Q2829|WD=Q2829}}) }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ooi Shrine''' (おおいじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine in Oi-cho, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}. It is a shikinaisha and was formerly ranked as a gosha. == Enshrined deities == * {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto}} (also called '''Mii-no-kami''') === History === === Traditions === ; Carp legend A shrine tradition says that in 702 deities from {{ill|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302}} traveled upstream on the Oi River, first riding a turtle; when blocked by rapids near Hozu, they switched to a carp and reached Ariimoto-buchi in Kameoka, where a shrine was founded and later moved to the present site.''Kyoto-fu no Chimei,'' Ooi Shrine section.Shrine origin record. From this legend, carp are treated as sacred messengers at this shrine. Parishioners traditionally avoid eating carp and do not display carp streamers at Boys' Festival. ; Origin of the name "Ooi" A tradition says the Kameoka Basin was once a lake ("Ni no Umi"). After it drained, a large spring/well remained here, never drying even in droughts, and this "great well" became the origin of the name "Ooi." Because loss of this spring was feared to bring either flooding or drought, the well deity Kimata-no-kami (Mii-no-kami) was invited and enshrined.On-site explanatory board. The shrine explains that its name derives from "great well" (oi) rather than from the river weir (oi) near Arashiyama. === Historical outline === Shrine tradition attributes foundation to an imperial order of Empress Genmei in 710. The shrine appears in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' as "Ooi Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province." It burned in warfare in 1576 and was rebuilt in 1584 by order of {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}; construction commissioner {{ill|lt=Katagiri Katsumoto|Q1882775|WD=Q1882775}} is said to have donated a plaque reading "Shoichii Ooi Daimyojin." In 1873 it was ranked gosha, recognized as a shikinaisha in June 1877, and designated as a shinsen-heihaku offering shrine in March 1907. Ahead of the 1300th anniversary in 2010, the honden underwent full restoration (completed October 2009). === Precinct === An Amida Buddha statue in the precinct is said to be the former honji-butsu of Toko-ji, once the shrine's betto temple. File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), honden.jpg|Honden (restoration completed 2009) File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), haiden.jpg|Haiden File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) keidai.JPG|Precinct File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) amidabutsu.JPG|Amida statue === Subsidiary shrines === '''Within precinct''' * Tenmangu ({{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005}}) * Inari Shrine (Omiyahime-no-Mikoto) * Izumo Shrine ({{ill|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Onamuchi-no-Mikoto}}) * Matsuo Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamakui|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Oyamakui-no-Mikoto}}) * Ebisu Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611}}) * Atago Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hokuzuchi-no-Mikoto}}) * Ohara Shrine ({{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}}) * Itsukushima Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tagorihime|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto}}) * Kasuga Shrine ({{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}) '''Outside precinct''' * Ooi Shrine (Kawarabayashi-cho, Kameoka) - former shrine site === Festivals === * Monthly festival (1st of each month) * New Year festival (Jan 1) * Traffic safety prayer (Jan 3) * Tondo fire rite (Jan 15) * Kinen-sai (spring equinox in March) * Summer purification (Jun 30) * Flower/Tachibana dedication rite (Aug 19) - Kyoto Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property * Grand festival / shinko-sai (Oct 16) : Horse-racing dedication (kurabe-uma) by parishioners in warrior attire is held at adjacent grounds. * Shichi-go-san prayers (November) * Niiname-sai (Nov 23) * Year-end purification (Dec 31) === Cultural properties === === Kyoto Prefecture designated cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural property: Ooi Shrine Tachibana rite (designated April 19, 1991) === Site information === '''Address''' * Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}} '''Access''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125}} {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011}} {{ill|lt=Namikawa Station|Q1044211|WD=Q1044211}} (about 4 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Commonscat|Ooi-jinja (Kameoka)}} * Shrine pamphlet "Guide to Ooi Shrine" * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture'' (Heibonsha), Ooi Shrine entry === External links === * {{Official website|https://ooijinja.blog.jp/|Ooi Shrine}} - Torii | location = {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996}} Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|53.5|N|135|33|25.0|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ooi Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} | founded = (trad.) Wado 3 (710) | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963}} | rituals = Tachibana rite ({{ill|lt=August 19|Q2829|WD=Q2829}}) }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ooi Shrine''' (おおいじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine in Oi-cho, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}. It is a shikinaisha and was formerly ranked as a gosha. == Enshrined deities == * {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto}} (also called '''Mii-no-kami''') === History === === Traditions === ; Carp legend A shrine tradition says that in 702 deities from {{ill|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302}} traveled upstream on the Oi River, first riding a turtle; when blocked by rapids near Hozu, they switched to a carp and reached Ariimoto-buchi in Kameoka, where a shrine was founded and later moved to the present site.''Kyoto-fu no Chimei,'' Ooi Shrine section.Shrine origin record. From this legend, carp are treated as sacred messengers at this shrine. Parishioners traditionally avoid eating carp and do not display carp streamers at Boys' Festival. ; Origin of the name "Ooi" A tradition says the Kameoka Basin was once a lake ("Ni no Umi"). After it drained, a large spring/well remained here, never drying even in droughts, and this "great well" became the origin of the name "Ooi." Because loss of this spring was feared to bring either flooding or drought, the well deity Kimata-no-kami (Mii-no-kami) was invited and enshrined.On-site explanatory board. The shrine explains that its name derives from "great well" (oi) rather than from the river weir (oi) near Arashiyama. === Historical outline === Shrine tradition attributes foundation to an imperial order of Empress Genmei in 710. The shrine appears in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' as "Ooi Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province." It burned in warfare in 1576 and was rebuilt in 1584 by order of {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}; construction commissioner {{ill|lt=Katagiri Katsumoto|Q1882775|WD=Q1882775}} is said to have donated a plaque reading "Shoichii Ooi Daimyojin." In 1873 it was ranked gosha, recognized as a shikinaisha in June 1877, and designated as a shinsen-heihaku offering shrine in March 1907. Ahead of the 1300th anniversary in 2010, the honden underwent full restoration (completed October 2009). === Precinct === An Amida Buddha statue in the precinct is said to be the former honji-butsu of Toko-ji, once the shrine's betto temple. File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), honden.jpg|Honden (restoration completed 2009) File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), haiden.jpg|Haiden File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) keidai.JPG|Precinct File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) amidabutsu.JPG|Amida statue === Subsidiary shrines === '''Within precinct''' * Tenmangu ({{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005}}) * Inari Shrine (Omiyahime-no-Mikoto) * Izumo Shrine ({{ill|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Onamuchi-no-Mikoto}}) * Matsuo Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamakui|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Oyamakui-no-Mikoto}}) * Ebisu Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611}}) * Atago Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hokuzuchi-no-Mikoto}}) * Ohara Shrine ({{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}}) * Itsukushima Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tagorihime|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto}}) * Kasuga Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}) '''Outside precinct''' * Ooi Shrine (Kawarabayashi-cho, Kameoka) - Wado 3 (710) | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963}} | rituals = Tachibana rite ({{ill|lt=August 19|Q2829|WD=Q2829}}) }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ooi Shrine''' (おおいじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine in Oi-cho, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}. It is a shikinaisha and was formerly ranked as a gosha. == Enshrined deities == * {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto}} (also called '''Mii-no-kami''') === History === === Traditions === ; Carp legend A shrine tradition says that in 702 deities from {{ill|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302}} traveled upstream on the Oi River, first riding a turtle; when blocked by rapids near Hozu, they switched to a carp and reached Ariimoto-buchi in Kameoka, where a shrine was founded and later moved to the present site.''Kyoto-fu no Chimei,'' Ooi Shrine section.Shrine origin record. From this legend, carp are treated as sacred messengers at this shrine. Parishioners traditionally avoid eating carp and do not display carp streamers at Boys' Festival. ; Origin of the name "Ooi" A tradition says the Kameoka Basin was once a lake ("Ni no Umi"). After it drained, a large spring/well remained here, never drying even in droughts, and this "great well" became the origin of the name "Ooi." Because loss of this spring was feared to bring either flooding or drought, the well deity Kimata-no-kami (Mii-no-kami) was invited and enshrined.On-site explanatory board. The shrine explains that its name derives from "great well" (oi) rather than from the river weir (oi) near Arashiyama. === Historical outline === Shrine tradition attributes foundation to an imperial order of Empress Genmei in 710. The shrine appears in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' as "Ooi Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province." It burned in warfare in 1576 and was rebuilt in 1584 by order of {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}; construction commissioner {{ill|lt=Katagiri Katsumoto|Q1882775|WD=Q1882775}} is said to have donated a plaque reading "Shoichii Ooi Daimyojin." In 1873 it was ranked gosha, recognized as a shikinaisha in June 1877, and designated as a shinsen-heihaku offering shrine in March 1907. Ahead of the 1300th anniversary in 2010, the honden underwent full restoration (completed October 2009). === Precinct === An Amida Buddha statue in the precinct is said to be the former honji-butsu of Toko-ji, once the shrine's betto temple. File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), honden.jpg|Honden (restoration completed 2009) File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), haiden.jpg|Haiden File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) keidai.JPG|Precinct File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) amidabutsu.JPG|Amida statue === Subsidiary shrines === '''Within precinct''' * Tenmangu ({{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005}}) * Inari Shrine (Omiyahime-no-Mikoto) * Izumo Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Onamuchi-no-Mikoto}}) * Matsuo Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamakui|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Oyamakui-no-Mikoto}}) * Ebisu Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611}}) * Atago Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hokuzuchi-no-Mikoto}}) * Ohara Shrine ({{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}}) * Itsukushima Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tagorihime|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto}}) * Kasuga Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}) '''Outside precinct''' * Ooi Shrine (Kawarabayashi-cho, Kameoka) - former shrine site === Festivals === * Monthly festival (1st of each month) * New Year festival (Jan 1) * Traffic safety prayer (Jan 3) * Tondo fire rite (Jan 15) * Kinen-sai (spring equinox in March) * Summer purification (Jun 30) * Flower/Tachibana dedication rite (Aug 19) - Kyoto Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property * Grand festival / shinko-sai (Oct 16) : Horse-racing dedication (kurabe-uma) by parishioners in warrior attire is held at adjacent grounds. * Shichi-go-san prayers (November) * Niiname-sai (Nov 23) * Year-end purification (Dec 31) === Cultural properties === === Kyoto Prefecture designated cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural property: Ooi Shrine Tachibana rite (designated April 19, 1991) === Site information === '''Address''' * Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}} '''Access''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125}} {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011}} {{ill|lt=Namikawa Station|Q1044211|WD=Q1044211}} (about 4 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Commonscat|Ooi-jinja (Kameoka)}} * Shrine pamphlet "Guide to Ooi Shrine" * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture'' (Heibonsha), Ooi Shrine entry === External links === * {{Official website|https://ooijinja.blog.jp/|Ooi Shrine}} - official site {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおいしんしやかめおかし}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ooi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ooi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:亀岡市の歴史]] [[Category:丹波国の式内社]] [[Category:京都府の旧郷社]] [[Category:丹波湖伝説]] [[Category:亀岡市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

== Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Ooi Shrine | image = [[File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), entrance.jpg|260px]]
Torii | location = {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}} Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|53.5|N|135|33|25.0|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ooi Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}}
{{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | founded = (trad.) Wado 3 (710) | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}} | rituals = Tachibana rite ({{ill|lt=August 19|Q2829|WD=Q2829|1=August 19}}) }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ooi Shrine''' (おおいじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine in Oi-cho, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}. It is a shikinaisha and was formerly ranked as a gosha. == Enshrined deities == * {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} (also called '''Mii-no-kami''') === History === === Traditions === ; Carp legend A shrine tradition says that in 702 deities from {{ill|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} traveled upstream on the Oi River, first riding a turtle; when blocked by rapids near Hozu, they switched to a carp and reached Ariimoto-buchi in Kameoka, where a shrine was founded and later moved to the present site.''Kyoto-fu no Chimei,'' Ooi Shrine section.Shrine origin record. From this legend, carp are treated as sacred messengers at this shrine. Parishioners traditionally avoid eating carp and do not display carp streamers at Boys' Festival. ; Origin of the name "Ooi" A tradition says the Kameoka Basin was once a lake ("Ni no Umi"). After it drained, a large spring/well remained here, never drying even in droughts, and this "great well" became the origin of the name "Ooi." Because loss of this spring was feared to bring either flooding or drought, the well deity Kimata-no-kami (Mii-no-kami) was invited and enshrined.On-site explanatory board. The shrine explains that its name derives from "great well" (oi) rather than from the river weir (oi) near Arashiyama. === Historical outline === Shrine tradition attributes foundation to an imperial order of Empress Genmei in 710. The shrine appears in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' as "Ooi Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province." It burned in warfare in 1576 and was rebuilt in 1584 by order of {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; construction commissioner {{ill|lt=Katagiri Katsumoto|Q1882775|WD=Q1882775|1=Katagiri Katsumoto}} is said to have donated a plaque reading "Shoichii Ooi Daimyojin." In 1873 it was ranked gosha, recognized as a shikinaisha in June 1877, and designated as a shinsen-heihaku offering shrine in March 1907. Ahead of the 1300th anniversary in 2010, the honden underwent full restoration (completed October 2009). === Precinct === An Amida Buddha statue in the precinct is said to be the former honji-butsu of Toko-ji, once the shrine's betto temple. File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), honden.jpg|Honden (restoration completed 2009) File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), haiden.jpg|Haiden File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) keidai.JPG|Precinct File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) amidabutsu.JPG|Amida statue === Subsidiary shrines === '''Within precinct''' * Tenmangu ({{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}) * Inari Shrine (Omiyahime-no-Mikoto) * Izumo Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Onamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}) * Matsuo Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamakui|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Oyamakui-no-Mikoto|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) * Ebisu Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}}) * Atago Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hokuzuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Ohara Shrine ({{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}) * Itsukushima Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tagorihime|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto}}) * Kasuga Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}}) '''Outside precinct''' * Ooi Shrine (Kawarabayashi-cho, Kameoka) - former shrine site === Festivals === * Monthly festival (1st of each month) * New Year festival (Jan 1) * Traffic safety prayer (Jan 3) * Tondo fire rite (Jan 15) * Kinen-sai (spring equinox in March) * Summer purification (Jun 30) * Flower/Tachibana dedication rite (Aug 19) - Kyoto Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property * Grand festival / shinko-sai (Oct 16) : Horse-racing dedication (kurabe-uma) by parishioners in warrior attire is held at adjacent grounds. * Shichi-go-san prayers (November) * Niiname-sai (Nov 23) * Year-end purification (Dec 31) === Cultural properties === === Kyoto Prefecture designated cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural property: Ooi Shrine Tachibana rite (designated April 19, 1991) === Site information === '''Address''' * Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} '''Access''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011|1=Sagano Line}} {{ill|lt=Namikawa Station|Q1044211|WD=Q1044211|1=Namikawa Station}} (about 4 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Commonscat|Ooi-jinja (Kameoka)}} * Shrine pamphlet "Guide to Ooi Shrine" * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture'' (Heibonsha), Ooi Shrine entry === External links === * {{Official website|https://ooijinja.blog.jp/|Ooi Shrine}} - In 1873 it was ranked gosha, recognized as a shikinaisha in June 1877, and designated as a shinsen-heihaku offering shrine in March 1907. Ahead of the 1300th anniversary in 2010, the honden underwent full restoration (completed October 2009). === Precinct === An Amida Buddha statue in the precinct is said to be the former honji-butsu of Toko-ji, once the shrine's betto temple. {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | founded = (trad.) Wado 3 (710) | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}} | rituals = Tachibana rite ({{ill|lt=August 19|Q2829|WD=Q2829|1=August 19}}) }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ooi Shrine''' (おおいじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine in Oi-cho, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}. It is a shikinaisha and was formerly ranked as a gosha. == Enshrined deities == * {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} (also called '''Mii-no-kami''') === History === === Traditions === ; Carp legend A shrine tradition says that in 702 deities from {{ill|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} traveled upstream on the Oi River, first riding a turtle; when blocked by rapids near Hozu, they switched to a carp and reached Ariimoto-buchi in Kameoka, where a shrine was founded and later moved to the present site.''Kyoto-fu no Chimei,'' Ooi Shrine section.Shrine origin record. From this legend, carp are treated as sacred messengers at this shrine. Parishioners traditionally avoid eating carp and do not display carp streamers at Boys' Festival. ; Origin of the name "Ooi" A tradition says the Kameoka Basin was once a lake ("Ni no Umi"). After it drained, a large spring/well remained here, never drying even in droughts, and this "great well" became the origin of the name "Ooi." Because loss of this spring was feared to bring either flooding or drought, the well deity Kimata-no-kami (Mii-no-kami) was invited and enshrined.On-site explanatory board. The shrine explains that its name derives from "great well" (oi) rather than from the river weir (oi) near Arashiyama. === Historical outline === Shrine tradition attributes foundation to an imperial order of Empress Genmei in 710. The shrine appears in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' as "Ooi Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province." It burned in warfare in 1576 and was rebuilt in 1584 by order of {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; construction commissioner {{ill|lt=Katagiri Katsumoto|Q1882775|WD=Q1882775|1=Katagiri Katsumoto}} is said to have donated a plaque reading "Shoichii Ooi Daimyojin." In 1873 it was ranked gosha, recognized as a shikinaisha in June 1877, and designated as a shinsen-heihaku offering shrine in March 1907. Ahead of the 1300th anniversary in 2010, the honden underwent full restoration (completed October 2009). === Precinct === An Amida Buddha statue in the precinct is said to be the former honji-butsu of Toko-ji, once the shrine's betto temple. == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Ooi Shrine | image = [[File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), entrance.jpg|260px]]
Torii | location = {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}} Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|53.5|N|135|33|25.0|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ooi Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}}
{{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | founded = (trad.) Wado 3 (710) | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}} | rituals = Tachibana rite ({{ill|lt=August 19|Q2829|WD=Q2829|1=August 19}}) }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ooi Shrine''' (おおいじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine in Oi-cho, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}. It is a shikinaisha and was formerly ranked as a gosha. == Enshrined deities == * {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} (also called '''Mii-no-kami''') === History === === Traditions === ; Carp legend A shrine tradition says that in 702 deities from {{ill|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} traveled upstream on the Oi River, first riding a turtle; when blocked by rapids near Hozu, they switched to a carp and reached Ariimoto-buchi in Kameoka, where a shrine was founded and later moved to the present site.''Kyoto-fu no Chimei,'' Ooi Shrine section.Shrine origin record. From this legend, carp are treated as sacred messengers at this shrine. Parishioners traditionally avoid eating carp and do not display carp streamers at Boys' Festival. ; Origin of the name "Ooi" A tradition says the Kameoka Basin was once a lake ("Ni no Umi"). After it drained, a large spring/well remained here, never drying even in droughts, and this "great well" became the origin of the name "Ooi." Because loss of this spring was feared to bring either flooding or drought, the well deity Kimata-no-kami (Mii-no-kami) was invited and enshrined.On-site explanatory board. The shrine explains that its name derives from "great well" (oi) rather than from the river weir (oi) near Arashiyama. === Historical outline === Shrine tradition attributes foundation to an imperial order of Empress Genmei in 710. The shrine appears in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' as "Ooi Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province." It burned in warfare in 1576 and was rebuilt in 1584 by order of {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; construction commissioner {{ill|lt=Katagiri Katsumoto|Q1882775|WD=Q1882775|1=Katagiri Katsumoto}} is said to have donated a plaque reading "Shoichii Ooi Daimyojin." In 1873 it was ranked gosha, recognized as a shikinaisha in June 1877, and designated as a shinsen-heihaku offering shrine in March 1907. Ahead of the 1300th anniversary in 2010, the honden underwent full restoration (completed October 2009). === Precinct === An Amida Buddha statue in the precinct is said to be the former honji-butsu of Toko-ji, once the shrine's betto temple. File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), honden.jpg|Honden (restoration completed 2009) File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), haiden.jpg|Haiden File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) keidai.JPG|Precinct File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) amidabutsu.JPG|Amida statue === Subsidiary shrines === '''Within precinct''' * Tenmangu ({{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}) * Inari Shrine (Omiyahime-no-Mikoto) * Izumo Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Onamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}) * Matsuo Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamakui|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Oyamakui-no-Mikoto|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) * Ebisu Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}}) * Atago Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hokuzuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Ohara Shrine ({{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}) * Itsukushima Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tagorihime|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto}}) * Kasuga Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}}) '''Outside precinct''' * Ooi Shrine (Kawarabayashi-cho, Kameoka) - former shrine site === Festivals === * Monthly festival (1st of each month) * New Year festival (Jan 1) * Traffic safety prayer (Jan 3) * Tondo fire rite (Jan 15) * Kinen-sai (spring equinox in March) * Summer purification (Jun 30) * Flower/Tachibana dedication rite (Aug 19) - Kyoto Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property * Grand festival / shinko-sai (Oct 16) : Horse-racing dedication (kurabe-uma) by parishioners in warrior attire is held at adjacent grounds. * Shichi-go-san prayers (November) * Niiname-sai (Nov 23) * Year-end purification (Dec 31) === Cultural properties === === Kyoto Prefecture designated cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural property: Ooi Shrine Tachibana rite (designated April 19, 1991) === Site information === '''Address''' * Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} '''Access''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011|1=Sagano Line}} {{ill|lt=Namikawa Station|Q1044211|WD=Q1044211|1=Namikawa Station}} (about 4 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Commonscat|Ooi-jinja (Kameoka)}} * Shrine pamphlet "Guide to Ooi Shrine" * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture'' (Heibonsha), Ooi Shrine entry === External links === * {{Official website|https://ooijinja.blog.jp/|Ooi Shrine}} - official site {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおいしんしやかめおかし}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ooi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ooi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:亀岡市の歴史]] [[Category:丹波国の式内社]] [[Category:京都府の旧郷社]] [[Category:丹波湖伝説]] [[Category:亀岡市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqqqqqq]]

== Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Ooi Shrine | image = [[File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), entrance.jpg|260px]]
Torii | location = {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}} Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|53.5|N|135|33|25.0|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ooi Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}}
{{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | founded = (trad.) Wado 3 (710) | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}} | rituals = Tachibana rite ({{ill|lt=August 19|Q2829|WD=Q2829|1=August 19}}) }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ooi Shrine''' (おおいじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine in Oi-cho, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}. It is a shikinaisha and was formerly ranked as a gosha. == Enshrined deities == * {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} (also called '''Mii-no-kami''') === History === === Traditions === ; Carp legend A shrine tradition says that in 702 deities from {{ill|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} traveled upstream on the Oi River, first riding a turtle; when blocked by rapids near Hozu, they switched to a carp and reached Ariimoto-buchi in Kameoka, where a shrine was founded and later moved to the present site.''Kyoto-fu no Chimei,'' Ooi Shrine section.Shrine origin record. From this legend, carp are treated as sacred messengers at this shrine. Parishioners traditionally avoid eating carp and do not display carp streamers at Boys' Festival. ; Origin of the name "Ooi" A tradition says the Kameoka Basin was once a lake ("Ni no Umi"). After it drained, a large spring/well remained here, never drying even in droughts, and this "great well" became the origin of the name "Ooi." Because loss of this spring was feared to bring either flooding or drought, the well deity Kimata-no-kami (Mii-no-kami) was invited and enshrined.On-site explanatory board. The shrine explains that its name derives from "great well" (oi) rather than from the river weir (oi) near Arashiyama. === Historical outline === Shrine tradition attributes foundation to an imperial order of Empress Genmei in 710. The shrine appears in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' as "Ooi Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province." It burned in warfare in 1576 and was rebuilt in 1584 by order of {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; construction commissioner {{ill|lt=Katagiri Katsumoto|Q1882775|WD=Q1882775|1=Katagiri Katsumoto}} is said to have donated a plaque reading "Shoichii Ooi Daimyojin." In 1873 it was ranked gosha, recognized as a shikinaisha in June 1877, and designated as a shinsen-heihaku offering shrine in March 1907. Ahead of the 1300th anniversary in 2010, the honden underwent full restoration (completed October 2009). === Precinct === An Amida Buddha statue in the precinct is said to be the former honji-butsu of Toko-ji, once the shrine's betto temple. File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), honden.jpg|Honden (restoration completed 2009) File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), haiden.jpg|Haiden File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) keidai.JPG|Precinct File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) amidabutsu.JPG|Amida statue === Subsidiary shrines === '''Within precinct''' * Tenmangu ({{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}) * Inari Shrine (Omiyahime-no-Mikoto) * Izumo Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Onamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}) * Matsuo Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamakui|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Oyamakui-no-Mikoto|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) * Ebisu Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}}) * Atago Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hokuzuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Ohara Shrine ({{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}) * Itsukushima Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tagorihime|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto}}) * Kasuga Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}}) '''Outside precinct''' * Ooi Shrine (Kawarabayashi-cho, Kameoka) - former shrine site === Festivals === * Monthly festival (1st of each month) * New Year festival (Jan 1) * Traffic safety prayer (Jan 3) * Tondo fire rite (Jan 15) * Kinen-sai (spring equinox in March) * Summer purification (Jun 30) * Flower/Tachibana dedication rite (Aug 19) - Kyoto Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property * Grand festival / shinko-sai (Oct 16) : Horse-racing dedication (kurabe-uma) by parishioners in warrior attire is held at adjacent grounds. * Shichi-go-san prayers (November) * Niiname-sai (Nov 23) * Year-end purification (Dec 31) === Cultural properties === === Kyoto Prefecture designated cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural property: Ooi Shrine Tachibana rite (designated April 19, 1991) === Site information === '''Address''' * Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} '''Access''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011|1=Sagano Line}} {{ill|lt=Namikawa Station|Q1044211|WD=Q1044211|1=Namikawa Station}} (about 4 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Commonscat|Ooi-jinja (Kameoka)}} * Shrine pamphlet "Guide to Ooi Shrine" * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture'' (Heibonsha), Ooi Shrine entry === External links === * {{Official website|https://ooijinja.blog.jp/|Ooi Shrine}} - == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Ooi Shrine | image = [[File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), entrance.jpg|260px]]
Torii | location = {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}} Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|53.5|N|135|33|25.0|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ooi Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}}
{{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | founded = (trad.) Wado 3 (710) | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}} | rituals = Tachibana rite ({{ill|lt=August 19|Q2829|WD=Q2829|1=August 19}}) }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ooi Shrine''' (おおいじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine in Oi-cho, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}. It is a shikinaisha and was formerly ranked as a gosha. == Enshrined deities == * {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} (also called '''Mii-no-kami''') === History === === Traditions === ; Carp legend A shrine tradition says that in 702 deities from {{ill|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} traveled upstream on the Oi River, first riding a turtle; when blocked by rapids near Hozu, they switched to a carp and reached Ariimoto-buchi in Kameoka, where a shrine was founded and later moved to the present site.''Kyoto-fu no Chimei,'' Ooi Shrine section.Shrine origin record. From this legend, carp are treated as sacred messengers at this shrine. Parishioners traditionally avoid eating carp and do not display carp streamers at Boys' Festival. ; Origin of the name "Ooi" A tradition says the Kameoka Basin was once a lake ("Ni no Umi"). After it drained, a large spring/well remained here, never drying even in droughts, and this "great well" became the origin of the name "Ooi." Because loss of this spring was feared to bring either flooding or drought, the well deity Kimata-no-kami (Mii-no-kami) was invited and enshrined.On-site explanatory board. The shrine explains that its name derives from "great well" (oi) rather than from the river weir (oi) near Arashiyama. === Historical outline === Shrine tradition attributes foundation to an imperial order of Empress Genmei in 710. The shrine appears in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' as "Ooi Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province." It burned in warfare in 1576 and was rebuilt in 1584 by order of {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; construction commissioner {{ill|lt=Katagiri Katsumoto|Q1882775|WD=Q1882775|1=Katagiri Katsumoto}} is said to have donated a plaque reading "Shoichii Ooi Daimyojin." In 1873 it was ranked gosha, recognized as a shikinaisha in June 1877, and designated as a shinsen-heihaku offering shrine in March 1907. Ahead of the 1300th anniversary in 2010, the honden underwent full restoration (completed October 2009). === Precinct === An Amida Buddha statue in the precinct is said to be the former honji-butsu of Toko-ji, once the shrine's betto temple. File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), honden.jpg|Honden (restoration completed 2009) File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), haiden.jpg|Haiden File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) keidai.JPG|Precinct File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) amidabutsu.JPG|Amida statue === Subsidiary shrines === '''Within precinct''' * Tenmangu ({{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}) * Inari Shrine (Omiyahime-no-Mikoto) * Izumo Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Onamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}) * Matsuo Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamakui|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Oyamakui-no-Mikoto|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) * Ebisu Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}}) * Atago Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hokuzuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Ohara Shrine ({{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}) * Itsukushima Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tagorihime|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto}}) * Kasuga Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}}) '''Outside precinct''' * Ooi Shrine (Kawarabayashi-cho, Kameoka) - former shrine site === Festivals === * Monthly festival (1st of each month) * New Year festival (Jan 1) * Traffic safety prayer (Jan 3) * Tondo fire rite (Jan 15) * Kinen-sai (spring equinox in March) * Summer purification (Jun 30) * Flower/Tachibana dedication rite (Aug 19) - Kyoto Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property * Grand festival / shinko-sai (Oct 16) : Horse-racing dedication (kurabe-uma) by parishioners in warrior attire is held at adjacent grounds. * Shichi-go-san prayers (November) * Niiname-sai (Nov 23) * Year-end purification (Dec 31) === Cultural properties === === Kyoto Prefecture designated cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural property: Ooi Shrine Tachibana rite (designated April 19, 1991) === Site information === '''Address''' * Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} '''Access''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011|1=Sagano Line}} {{ill|lt=Namikawa Station|Q1044211|WD=Q1044211|1=Namikawa Station}} (about 4 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Commonscat|Ooi-jinja (Kameoka)}} * Shrine pamphlet "Guide to Ooi Shrine" * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture'' (Heibonsha), Ooi Shrine entry === External links === * {{Official website|https://ooijinja.blog.jp/|Ooi Shrine}} - official site {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ooi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ooi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:亀岡市の歴史]] [[Category:丹波国の式内社]] [[Category:京都府の旧郷社]] [[Category:丹波湖伝説]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameoka, Kyoto]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqqqqqq]]

== Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Ooi Shrine | image = [[File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), entrance.jpg|260px]]
Torii | location = {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}} Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|53.5|N|135|33|25.0|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ooi Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}}
{{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | founded = (trad.) Wado 3 (710) | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}} | rituals = Tachibana rite ({{ill|lt=August 19|Q2829|WD=Q2829|1=August 19}}) }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ooi Shrine''' (おおいじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine in Oi-cho, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}. It is a shikinaisha and was formerly ranked as a gosha. == Enshrined deities == * {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} (also called '''Mii-no-kami''') === History === === Traditions === ; Carp legend A shrine tradition says that in 702 deities from {{ill|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} traveled upstream on the Oi River, first riding a turtle; when blocked by rapids near Hozu, they switched to a carp and reached Ariimoto-buchi in Kameoka, where a shrine was founded and later moved to the present site.''Kyoto-fu no Chimei,'' Ooi Shrine section.Shrine origin record. From this legend, carp are treated as sacred messengers at this shrine. Parishioners traditionally avoid eating carp and do not display carp streamers at Boys' Festival. ; Origin of the name "Ooi" A tradition says the Kameoka Basin was once a lake ("Ni no Umi"). After it drained, a large spring/well remained here, never drying even in droughts, and this "great well" became the origin of the name "Ooi." Because loss of this spring was feared to bring either flooding or drought, the well deity Kimata-no-kami (Mii-no-kami) was invited and enshrined.On-site explanatory board. The shrine explains that its name derives from "great well" (oi) rather than from the river weir (oi) near Arashiyama. === Historical outline === Shrine tradition attributes foundation to an imperial order of Empress Genmei in 710. The shrine appears in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' as "Ooi Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province." It burned in warfare in 1576 and was rebuilt in 1584 by order of {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; construction commissioner {{ill|lt=Katagiri Katsumoto|Q1882775|WD=Q1882775|1=Katagiri Katsumoto}} is said to have donated a plaque reading "Shoichii Ooi Daimyojin." In 1873 it was ranked gosha, recognized as a shikinaisha in June 1877, and designated as a shinsen-heihaku offering shrine in March 1907. Ahead of the 1300th anniversary in 2010, the honden underwent full restoration (completed October 2009). === Precinct === An Amida Buddha statue in the precinct is said to be the former honji-butsu of Toko-ji, once the shrine's betto temple. File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), honden.jpg|Honden (restoration completed 2009) File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), haiden.jpg|Haiden File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) keidai.JPG|Precinct File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) amidabutsu.JPG|Amida statue === Subsidiary shrines === '''Within precinct''' * Tenmangu ({{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}) * Inari Shrine (Omiyahime-no-Mikoto) * Izumo Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Onamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}) * Matsuo Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamakui|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Oyamakui-no-Mikoto|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) * Ebisu Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}}) * Atago Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hokuzuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Ohara Shrine ({{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}) * Itsukushima Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tagorihime|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto}}) * Kasuga Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}}) '''Outside precinct''' * Ooi Shrine (Kawarabayashi-cho, Kameoka) - former shrine site === Festivals === * Monthly festival (1st of each month) * New Year festival (Jan 1) * Traffic safety prayer (Jan 3) * Tondo fire rite (Jan 15) * Kinen-sai (spring equinox in March) * Summer purification (Jun 30) * Flower/Tachibana dedication rite (Aug 19) - Kyoto Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property * Grand festival / shinko-sai (Oct 16) : Horse-racing dedication (kurabe-uma) by parishioners in warrior attire is held at adjacent grounds. * Shichi-go-san prayers (November) * Niiname-sai (Nov 23) * Year-end purification (Dec 31) === Cultural properties === === Kyoto Prefecture designated cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural property: Ooi Shrine Tachibana rite (designated April 19, 1991) === Site information === '''Address''' * Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} '''Access''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011|1=Sagano Line}} {{ill|lt=Namikawa Station|Q1044211|WD=Q1044211|1=Namikawa Station}} (about 4 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Commonscat|Ooi-jinja (Kameoka)}} * Shrine pamphlet "Guide to Ooi Shrine" * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture'' (Heibonsha), Ooi Shrine entry === External links === * {{Official website|https://ooijinja.blog.jp/|Ooi Shrine}} - {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | founded = (trad.) Wado 3 (710) | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}} | rituals = Tachibana rite ({{ill|lt=August 19|Q2829|WD=Q2829|1=August 19}}) }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ooi Shrine''' (おおいじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine in Oi-cho, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}. It is a shikinaisha and was formerly ranked as a gosha. == Enshrined deities == * {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} (also called '''Mii-no-kami''') === History === === Traditions === ; Carp legend A shrine tradition says that in 702 deities from {{ill|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} traveled upstream on the Oi River, first riding a turtle; when blocked by rapids near Hozu, they switched to a carp and reached Ariimoto-buchi in Kameoka, where a shrine was founded and later moved to the present site.''Kyoto-fu no Chimei,'' Ooi Shrine section.Shrine origin record. From this legend, carp are treated as sacred messengers at this shrine. Parishioners traditionally avoid eating carp and do not display carp streamers at Boys' Festival. ; Origin of the name "Ooi" A tradition says the Kameoka Basin was once a lake ("Ni no Umi"). After it drained, a large spring/well remained here, never drying even in droughts, and this "great well" became the origin of the name "Ooi." Because loss of this spring was feared to bring either flooding or drought, the well deity Kimata-no-kami (Mii-no-kami) was invited and enshrined.On-site explanatory board. The shrine explains that its name derives from "great well" (oi) rather than from the river weir (oi) near Arashiyama. === Historical outline === Shrine tradition attributes foundation to an imperial order of Empress Genmei in 710. The shrine appears in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' as "Ooi Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province." It burned in warfare in 1576 and was rebuilt in 1584 by order of {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; construction commissioner {{ill|lt=Katagiri Katsumoto|Q1882775|WD=Q1882775|1=Katagiri Katsumoto}} is said to have donated a plaque reading "Shoichii Ooi Daimyojin." In 1873 it was ranked gosha, recognized as a shikinaisha in June 1877, and designated as a shinsen-heihaku offering shrine in March 1907. Ahead of the 1300th anniversary in 2010, the honden underwent full restoration (completed October 2009). === Precinct === An Amida Buddha statue in the precinct is said to be the former honji-butsu of Toko-ji, once the shrine's betto temple. File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), honden.jpg|Honden (restoration completed 2009) File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), haiden.jpg|Haiden File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) keidai.JPG|Precinct File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) amidabutsu.JPG|Amida statue === Subsidiary shrines === '''Within precinct''' * Tenmangu ({{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}) * Inari Shrine (Omiyahime-no-Mikoto) * Izumo Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Onamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}) * Matsuo Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamakui|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Oyamakui-no-Mikoto|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) * Ebisu Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}}) * Atago Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hokuzuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Ohara Shrine ({{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}) * Itsukushima Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tagorihime|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto}}) * Kasuga Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}}) '''Outside precinct''' * Ooi Shrine (Kawarabayashi-cho, Kameoka) - former shrine site === Festivals === * Monthly festival (1st of each month) * New Year festival (Jan 1) * Traffic safety prayer (Jan 3) * Tondo fire rite (Jan 15) * Kinen-sai (spring equinox in March) * Summer purification (Jun 30) * Flower/Tachibana dedication rite (Aug 19) - Kyoto Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property * Grand festival / shinko-sai (Oct 16) : Horse-racing dedication (kurabe-uma) by parishioners in warrior attire is held at adjacent grounds. * Shichi-go-san prayers (November) * Niiname-sai (Nov 23) * Year-end purification (Dec 31) === Cultural properties === === Kyoto Prefecture designated cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural property: Ooi Shrine Tachibana rite (designated April 19, 1991) === Site information === '''Address''' * Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} '''Access''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011|1=Sagano Line}} {{ill|lt=Namikawa Station|Q1044211|WD=Q1044211|1=Namikawa Station}} (about 4 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Commonscat|Ooi-jinja (Kameoka)}} * Shrine pamphlet "Guide to Ooi Shrine" * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture'' (Heibonsha), Ooi Shrine entry === External links === * {{Official website|https://ooijinja.blog.jp/|Ooi Shrine}} - It is a shikinaisha and was formerly ranked as a gosha. == Enshrined deities == * {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} (also called '''Mii-no-kami''') === History === === Traditions === ; Carp legend A shrine tradition says that in 702 deities from {{ill|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} traveled upstream on the Oi River, first riding a turtle; when blocked by rapids near Hozu, they switched to a carp and reached Ariimoto-buchi in Kameoka, where a shrine was founded and later moved to the present site.''Kyoto-fu no Chimei,'' Ooi Shrine section.Shrine origin record. From this legend, carp are treated as sacred messengers at this shrine. Parishioners traditionally avoid eating carp and do not display carp streamers at Boys' Festival. ; Origin of the name "Ooi" A tradition says the Kameoka Basin was once a lake ("Ni no Umi"). After it drained, a large spring/well remained here, never drying even in droughts, and this "great well" became the origin of the name "Ooi." Because loss of this spring was feared to bring either flooding or drought, the well deity Kimata-no-kami (Mii-no-kami) was invited and enshrined.On-site explanatory board. The shrine explains that its name derives from "great well" (oi) rather than from the river weir (oi) near Arashiyama. === Historical outline === Shrine tradition attributes foundation to an imperial order of Empress Genmei in 710. The shrine appears in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' as "Ooi Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province." It burned in warfare in 1576 and was rebuilt in 1584 by order of {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; construction commissioner {{ill|lt=Katagiri Katsumoto|Q1882775|WD=Q1882775|1=Katagiri Katsumoto}} is said to have donated a plaque reading "Shoichii Ooi Daimyojin." In 1873 it was ranked gosha, recognized as a shikinaisha in June 1877, and designated as a shinsen-heihaku offering shrine in March 1907. Ahead of the 1300th anniversary in 2010, the honden underwent full restoration (completed October 2009). === Precinct === An Amida Buddha statue in the precinct is said to be the former honji-butsu of Toko-ji, once the shrine's betto temple. Kyoto Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property * Grand festival / shinko-sai (Oct 16) : Horse-racing dedication (kurabe-uma) by parishioners in warrior attire is held at adjacent grounds. * Shichi-go-san prayers (November) * Niiname-sai (Nov 23) * Year-end purification (Dec 31) === Cultural properties === === Kyoto Prefecture designated cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural property: Ooi Shrine Tachibana rite (designated April 19, 1991) === Site information === '''Address''' * Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} '''Access''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011|1=Sagano Line}} {{ill|lt=Namikawa Station|Q1044211|WD=Q1044211|1=Namikawa Station}} (about 4 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Commonscat|Ooi-jinja (Kameoka)}} * Shrine pamphlet "Guide to Ooi Shrine" * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture'' (Heibonsha), Ooi Shrine entry === External links === * {{Official website|https://ooijinja.blog.jp/|Ooi Shrine}} - == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Ooi Shrine | image = [[File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), entrance.jpg|260px]]
Torii | location = {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}} Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|53.5|N|135|33|25.0|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ooi Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}}
{{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | founded = (trad.) Wado 3 (710) | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}} | rituals = Tachibana rite ({{ill|lt=August 19|Q2829|WD=Q2829|1=August 19}}) }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ooi Shrine''' (おおいじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine in Oi-cho, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}. It is a shikinaisha and was formerly ranked as a gosha. == Enshrined deities == * {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} (also called '''Mii-no-kami''') === History === === Traditions === ; Carp legend A shrine tradition says that in 702 deities from {{ill|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} traveled upstream on the Oi River, first riding a turtle; when blocked by rapids near Hozu, they switched to a carp and reached Ariimoto-buchi in Kameoka, where a shrine was founded and later moved to the present site.''Kyoto-fu no Chimei,'' Ooi Shrine section.Shrine origin record. From this legend, carp are treated as sacred messengers at this shrine. Parishioners traditionally avoid eating carp and do not display carp streamers at Boys' Festival. ; Origin of the name "Ooi" A tradition says the Kameoka Basin was once a lake ("Ni no Umi"). After it drained, a large spring/well remained here, never drying even in droughts, and this "great well" became the origin of the name "Ooi." Because loss of this spring was feared to bring either flooding or drought, the well deity Kimata-no-kami (Mii-no-kami) was invited and enshrined.On-site explanatory board. The shrine explains that its name derives from "great well" (oi) rather than from the river weir (oi) near Arashiyama. === Historical outline === Shrine tradition attributes foundation to an imperial order of Empress Genmei in 710. The shrine appears in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' as "Ooi Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province." It burned in warfare in 1576 and was rebuilt in 1584 by order of {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; construction commissioner {{ill|lt=Katagiri Katsumoto|Q1882775|WD=Q1882775|1=Katagiri Katsumoto}} is said to have donated a plaque reading "Shoichii Ooi Daimyojin." In 1873 it was ranked gosha, recognized as a shikinaisha in June 1877, and designated as a shinsen-heihaku offering shrine in March 1907. Ahead of the 1300th anniversary in 2010, the honden underwent full restoration (completed October 2009). === Precinct === An Amida Buddha statue in the precinct is said to be the former honji-butsu of Toko-ji, once the shrine's betto temple. File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), honden.jpg|Honden (restoration completed 2009) File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), haiden.jpg|Haiden File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) keidai.JPG|Precinct File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) amidabutsu.JPG|Amida statue === Subsidiary shrines === '''Within precinct''' * Tenmangu ({{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}) * Inari Shrine (Omiyahime-no-Mikoto) * Izumo Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Onamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}) * Matsuo Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamakui|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Oyamakui-no-Mikoto|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) * Ebisu Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}}) * Atago Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hokuzuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Ohara Shrine ({{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}) * Itsukushima Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tagorihime|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto}}) * Kasuga Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}}) '''Outside precinct''' * Ooi Shrine (Kawarabayashi-cho, Kameoka) - former shrine site === Festivals === * Monthly festival (1st of each month) * New Year festival (Jan 1) * Traffic safety prayer (Jan 3) * Tondo fire rite (Jan 15) * Kinen-sai (spring equinox in March) * Summer purification (Jun 30) * Flower/Tachibana dedication rite (Aug 19) - Kyoto Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property * Grand festival / shinko-sai (Oct 16) : Horse-racing dedication (kurabe-uma) by parishioners in warrior attire is held at adjacent grounds. * Shichi-go-san prayers (November) * Niiname-sai (Nov 23) * Year-end purification (Dec 31) === Cultural properties === === Kyoto Prefecture designated cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural property: Ooi Shrine Tachibana rite (designated April 19, 1991) === Site information === '''Address''' * Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} '''Access''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011|1=Sagano Line}} {{ill|lt=Namikawa Station|Q1044211|WD=Q1044211|1=Namikawa Station}} (about 4 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Commonscat|Ooi-jinja (Kameoka)}} * Shrine pamphlet "Guide to Ooi Shrine" * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture'' (Heibonsha), Ooi Shrine entry === External links === * {{Official website|https://ooijinja.blog.jp/|Ooi Shrine}} - official site {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ooi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ooi Shrine]][[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:亀岡市の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:丹波湖伝説]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameoka, Kyoto]]

{{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | founded = (trad.) Wado 3 (710) | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}} | rituals = Tachibana rite ({{ill|lt=August 19|Q2829|WD=Q2829|1=August 19}}) }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ooi Shrine''' (おおいじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine in Oi-cho, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}. It is a shikinaisha and was formerly ranked as a gosha. == Enshrined deities == * {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kimata-no-kami|Q11092833|WD=Q11092833|lt=Kimata-no-Mikoto|1=Kimata-no-Kami}} (also called '''Mii-no-kami''') === History === === Traditions === ; Carp legend A shrine tradition says that in 702 deities from {{ill|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} traveled upstream on the Oi River, first riding a turtle; when blocked by rapids near Hozu, they switched to a carp and reached Ariimoto-buchi in Kameoka, where a shrine was founded and later moved to the present site.''Kyoto-fu no Chimei,'' Ooi Shrine section.Shrine origin record. From this legend, carp are treated as sacred messengers at this shrine. Parishioners traditionally avoid eating carp and do not display carp streamers at Boys' Festival. ; Origin of the name "Ooi" A tradition says the Kameoka Basin was once a lake ("Ni no Umi"). After it drained, a large spring/well remained here, never drying even in droughts, and this "great well" became the origin of the name "Ooi." Because loss of this spring was feared to bring either flooding or drought, the well deity Kimata-no-kami (Mii-no-kami) was invited and enshrined.On-site explanatory board. The shrine explains that its name derives from "great well" (oi) rather than from the river weir (oi) near Arashiyama. === Historical outline === Shrine tradition attributes foundation to an imperial order of Empress Genmei in 710. The shrine appears in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' as "Ooi Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province." It burned in warfare in 1576 and was rebuilt in 1584 by order of {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; construction commissioner {{ill|lt=Katagiri Katsumoto|Q1882775|WD=Q1882775|1=Katagiri Katsumoto}} is said to have donated a plaque reading "Shoichii Ooi Daimyojin." In 1873 it was ranked gosha, recognized as a shikinaisha in June 1877, and designated as a shinsen-heihaku offering shrine in March 1907. Ahead of the 1300th anniversary in 2010, the honden underwent full restoration (completed October 2009). === Precinct === An Amida Buddha statue in the precinct is said to be the former honji-butsu of Toko-ji, once the shrine's betto temple. File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), honden.jpg|Honden (restoration completed 2009) File:Oi-jinja (Kameoka), haiden.jpg|Haiden File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) keidai.JPG|Precinct File:Ooi-jinja (Kameoka) amidabutsu.JPG|Amida statue === Subsidiary shrines === '''Within precinct''' * Tenmangu ({{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}) * Inari Shrine (Omiyahime-no-Mikoto) * Izumo Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Onamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}) * Matsuo Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamakui|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Oyamakui-no-Mikoto|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) * Ebisu Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}}) * Atago Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hokuzuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Ohara Shrine ({{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}) * Itsukushima Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tagorihime|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto}}) * Kasuga Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}}) '''Outside precinct''' * Ooi Shrine (Kawarabayashi-cho, Kameoka) - former shrine site === Festivals === * Monthly festival (1st of each month) * New Year festival (Jan 1) * Traffic safety prayer (Jan 3) * Tondo fire rite (Jan 15) * Kinen-sai (spring equinox in March) * Summer purification (Jun 30) * Flower/Tachibana dedication rite (Aug 19) - Kyoto Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property * Grand festival / shinko-sai (Oct 16) : Horse-racing dedication (kurabe-uma) by parishioners in warrior attire is held at adjacent grounds. * Shichi-go-san prayers (November) * Niiname-sai (Nov 23) * Year-end purification (Dec 31) === Cultural properties === === Kyoto Prefecture designated cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural property: Ooi Shrine Tachibana rite (designated April 19, 1991) === Site information === '''Address''' * Oi-cho Namikawa 1-3-25, {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} '''Access''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011|1=Sagano Line}} {{ill|lt=Namikawa Station|Q1044211|WD=Q1044211|1=Namikawa Station}} (about 4 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Commonscat|Ooi-jinja (Kameoka)}} * Shrine pamphlet "Guide to Ooi Shrine" * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture'' (Heibonsha), Ooi Shrine entry === External links === * {{Official website|https://ooijinja.blog.jp/|Ooi Shrine}} -
